  - Whether the brand convention in this market is to transliterate at all, or to keep the English form as a brand asset

CRITICAL FOR CHINESE TRANSLITERATION
For brand names entering Chinese markets, evaluate:
- Phonetic match
- Character meaning (the chosen characters should have positive or neutral connotations)
- Visual aesthetic

End with a single RECOMMENDATION for each market: transliterate, partial transliterate, or keep English.

input_variables.name: source
input_variables.example: OSS AI Hub

input_variables.name: source_language_script
input_variables.example: Latin (English)

input_variables.name: target_scripts
input_variables.example: Katakana, Hangul, Simplified Chinese, Cyrillic

input_variables.name: use_case
input_variables.description: How the transliteration will be used
input_variables.example: Brand name for marketing in JP, KR, CN, RU markets

expected_output.sample: One section per target script with primary, alternates, pronunciation, notes, and a market-level recommendation.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on character connotation analysis for Chinese.
Very good; occasionally misses regional alternates.
Solid; weaker on Chinese brand fit analysis.
Adequate for common names; weaker on rare characters.

variations.label: Phonetic-only
variations.description: Skip the brand fit analysis.
variations.prompt_snippet: Omit character connotations; output only phonetic transliteration + alternates.

variations.label: Cross-system table
variations.description: Single table across all scripts.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace per-script sections with a single comparison table: source | script | primary | alternates | notes.

variations.label: Brand-fit deep dive
variations.description: Chinese only, full brand analysis.
variations.prompt_snippet: Focus on Chinese: produce 3 candidate transliterations, each with character meaning, market positioning, and risk.

failure_modes.symptom: Only one variant given when multiple are standard.
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘when more than one is in active use, list all and note when each is preferred.’
Re-pin Chinese-specific rule; require explicit meaning gloss per character.

failure_modes.symptom: Transliteration that's phonetically right but visually clunky.
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘for brand use, optimize for visual + phonetic; for legal use, optimize for phonetic alignment to passport/ID.’

failure_modes.symptom: Mixed script transliteration (some hiragana, some katakana).
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘use the script that's culturally expected for the entity type (foreign brands = katakana; foreign person names = katakana; native concepts = mix accordingly).’

faq.question: Why are there multiple correct transliterations?
faq.answer: Different romanization/transliteration systems exist (Hepburn vs Kunrei-shiki for Japanese, Pinyin vs Wade-Giles for Chinese). Default to the most widely used in the target market today.

faq.question: Should my brand always transliterate?
faq.answer: No. Many global brands (Apple, Google, Sony) use English in some markets as a luxury/foreign-modern signal. Use the recommendation to decide per market.

faq.question: How do I avoid offensive character choices in Chinese?
faq.answer: Always validate with a native speaker. The model catches most issues but Chinese character connotations are dense; a final human check is essential for brand launches.

meta_title: Name and Brand Transliteration — Prompt
meta_description: Transliterate names and brand terms across writing systems with ranked variants, pronunciation guides, and brand-fit analysis for Chinese.

title: Subtitle Translator With Timing-Aware Compression
tldr: Translates subtitles (SRT/VTT) into a target language while compressing each line to fit the on-screen reading time — preserves meaning while respecting the 17–21 characters-per-second rule.

use_cases.example: Translate English SRT → Japanese SRT respecting 17 chars/sec reading speed.
Localize 20 hours of lectures; subtitle file generated per language.
Preserve dramatic timing of original; compress translation to fit cuts.
Live-captioned talk → polished bilingual subtitles.

when_not_to_use: Skip for dubbing or voiceover — those require lip-sync and voice acting considerations the subtitle prompt isn't built for.
full_prompt:
You are a subtitle translator. Translate SRT/VTT subtitles into the target language WITHOUT exceeding on-screen reading speed.

INPUT
- Source subtitles: {srt_or_vtt}
- Source language: {source_language}
- Target language: {target_language}
- Reading speed cap: {chars_per_second} characters per second (default 17)
- Max chars per line: {max_chars_per_line} (default 42)
- Max lines per cue: 2

YOUR RULES
1. Each cue keeps its timing. You do NOT split or merge cues.
2. Each cue's translation must fit within (cue_duration_seconds * chars_per_second).
3. If a literal translation would exceed the budget:
   - Compress: cut filler ("you know", "basically", "um"), tighten phrasing, use shorter synonyms.
   - If still over budget after compression, sacrifice nuance to keep core meaning.
4. Two-line cues: break at a natural pause point. Avoid splitting noun phrases.
5. Preserve emphasis cues: italics in source → italics in target.

OUTPUT
Return the translated SRT/VTT in valid format. Below the file, in a "Compression notes" section, list any cue where compression sacrificed meaning, with:
- Cue number
- Original (translated literally) — what it would have said
- Final (compressed) — what it actually says
- What was lost

Begin.

input_variables.name: srt_or_vtt
input_variables.description: SRT or VTT subtitle file contents
input_variables.example:
1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,000
Welcome to today's lecture on neural networks.

input_variables.name: source_language
input_variables.example: English

input_variables.name: target_language
input_variables.example: Japanese

expected_output.sample: Translated SRT/VTT file in target language, followed by a Compression notes section.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at respecting timing budget; useful compression notes.
Very good; occasionally exceeds chars/sec on long cues — verify.
Solid; struggles with two-line break decisions in Asian languages.
Acceptable for short videos; consistency issues on long files.

variations.label: Educational content
variations.description: Preserve technical terms unchanged.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘technical terms appearing in slides or chyrons remain in original language with localized explanation below if space allows.’

variations.label: Forced narratives only
variations.description: Translate only forced narratives.
variations.prompt_snippet: Filter input to only cues marked as forced (on-screen text translations); skip dialogue.

variations.label: Multi-language batch
variations.description: Same source → multiple target languages.
variations.prompt_snippet: Generate target SRTs for {languages}; each respects its own char/sec norm.

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin and add ‘verify cue_duration_seconds * chars_per_second BEFORE emitting; if over, compress further.’

failure_modes.symptom: Splits noun phrases across lines awkwardly.
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘line break must be at a natural pause; if no good break exists, rewrite to two shorter sentences.’
Force compression notes; tune chars/sec up if too aggressive.
Add: ‘output must validate as SRT — cue numbers, timestamps in HH:MM:SS,mmm --> HH:MM:SS,mmm, blank line between cues.’

faq.question: Why 17 chars per second?
faq.answer: Netflix's research-based standard for sustained reading comfort. BBC uses 15 for kids' content; tech/gaming creators sometimes push to 20.

faq.question: Does this work for any language pair?
faq.answer: Yes, but the char/sec budget should adjust: Chinese/Japanese readers are slower per character, so use lower caps (8–10 for kanji-heavy text).

faq.question: Can I run this on a 2-hour film?
faq.answer: Yes, but chunk by scene to avoid context loss. Concatenate the SRTs back at the end.

meta_title: Subtitle Translator With Timing-Aware Compression
meta_description: Translate SRT/VTT subtitles into a target language while respecting reading-speed caps and on-screen line limits — Netflix-grade subtitle output.

title: Newsletter Issue From The Week's Notes
tldr: Drafts a newsletter issue from raw weekly notes — picks the 2-3 strongest threads, finds the through-line, and lands one specific takeaway readers can act on Monday.

use_cases.example: Past 7 days of notes (Slack, calls, decisions) → one synthesized 800-word note with a clear thesis.
Raw notes from links, books, conversations → newsletter that has a point, not just a list.
Engineering team's week of work → digest framed for product/leadership readers.
Journal + screenshots → reader-friendly version with one concrete takeaway.

when_not_to_use: Skip when you actually need a roundup of links (use a curation prompt). Skip when the week didn't have a real through-line — forcing one is how newsletters get bad.
full_prompt:
You are drafting one issue of a weekly newsletter from the writer's raw notes. The goal is NOT a roundup; it's a piece with a point.

WHAT YOU GET
- Notes from the past week: meetings, articles read, decisions, observations, sketches of arguments.

WHAT YOU PRODUCE

## Step 1: Find the through-line
Read all the notes. What's the ONE thread that connects 2-3 of the strongest items? Don't pick the biggest news — pick the most cohesive idea. State it in one sentence.

## Step 2: Draft the issue
Length: {target_word_count} words. Structure:

1. OPENING (50-100 words). State the through-line — not as a thesis statement, but as a vivid moment or observation from the week. Don't bury the lead, but don't announce it either.

2. BODY (60% of word count). Develop the through-line using 2-3 specific moments from the notes. For each: concrete detail, what made it stick, what it implies.

3. THE TURN (10-15% of words). Where you complicate or push back on your own opening. Newsletters that don't earn this feel like LinkedIn posts.

4. CLOSING (1 short paragraph). The thing reader could DO on Monday. Specific. Concrete.

## Step 3: Subject line
3 candidates. Each:
- A specific noun, not a category.
- A real promise, not a tease.
- Under 60 characters.

VOICE NOTES
- Specific over general (one named person beats "people"; one Tuesday morning beats "this week").
- One idea well-developed beats three ideas mentioned.
- The reader is busy and smart — write for that.

CONSTRAINTS
- Use only what's in the notes. Don't invent supporting details.
- Don't write "delve", "dive into", "in today's fast-paced world", or any tone-marker of AI-written content.
- If two items in the notes contradict, name the contradiction explicitly.

RAW NOTES
{raw_notes}

Begin with Step 1.

input_variables.name: raw_notes
input_variables.example: Mon: call w/ Anita, talked about pricing experiment. Article: 'Why SaaS is hard now' (NYT). Wed: shipped feature. Realized our docs are stale...

expected_output.sample: Through-line statement, then drafted issue with opening, body, turn, closing — plus 3 subject lines.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at finding the actual through-line; doesn't force coherence on disparate notes.
Strong; sometimes pads body length — re-pin word count.
Often turns ‘the turn’ into a generic pivot; ask for genuine complication.
Falls into roundup format; needs constant reminder to find single thread.

variations.label: Single-link expansion
variations.description: Expand one strong link into the issue.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace through-line step with: ‘pick the single most resonant link/conversation; write the entire issue using that as the spine.’

variations.label: Photo-led
variations.description: Open with the week's strongest visual.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘open with a description of one image/moment from the week; let it anchor the through-line.’

variations.label: Q&A from a real reader
variations.description: Frame around a reader's question.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘open with a reader's question from this week; treat the issue as your considered answer.’

failure_modes.symptom: Output is a roundup with bullets instead of an issue.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin ‘not a roundup; one through-line’ and require ‘the turn’ paragraph explicitly.
Add: ‘through-line must reference at least 2 specific items from the notes.’

failure_modes.symptom: Closing CTA is vague (‘think about how this applies’).
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘closing names ONE concrete action the reader can take by next week.’
Add banned phrases list including ‘in today's fast-paced world’, ‘delve’, ‘dive deep’, ‘unlock’ (verb), ‘game-changer’.

faq.question: Why force ‘the turn’?
faq.answer: Without it, newsletters read as one-take pronouncements. The turn signals to readers you've sat with the idea long enough to see its other side.

faq.question: What if my week genuinely was a roundup?
faq.answer: Then write a roundup honestly. Don't manufacture a thesis when there isn't one — readers smell it.

faq.question: How is this different from a blog post?
faq.answer: Newsletter = intimate voice, time-bound, one idea. Blog post = standalone, evergreen, can be longer and more structured. The opening especially differs — newsletters open in scene; posts often open in argument.

meta_title: Newsletter Issue From The Week's Notes — Prompt
meta_description: Draft a newsletter issue with a real through-line: pick 2-3 strongest threads, develop them, add the turn, land one concrete takeaway.

title: LinkedIn Post With Narrative Arc
tldr: Turns a single insight into a LinkedIn post that actually has a story arc — setup, complication, turn, payoff — not a listicle, not a humble-brag.

use_cases.scenario: Founder writing about a lesson learned
use_cases.example: Real moment from the company → 5-paragraph LinkedIn post that builds toward the lesson, not announces it.
Apply the framework to a real client scenario; let the story carry the framework.

use_cases.scenario: Employee announcing a milestone (job change, anniversary)
use_cases.example: Personal but not cringey — anchored in specifics.

use_cases.scenario: Engineer reflecting on a project shipped
use_cases.example: Technical context made readable; honest about what went wrong.

when_not_to_use: Skip for sales posts (use a dedicated sales hook prompt). Skip when you don't have a real moment to anchor in — without specifics, the post will read fake.
full_prompt:
You are drafting a LinkedIn post that has a real narrative arc — not a listicle, not a humble-brag.

INPUT
- The CORE INSIGHT you want to land: {insight}
- The REAL MOMENT it came from (specific time, specific person, specific decision): {moment}
- AUDIENCE: {audience}
- VOICE NOTES (banned phrases, preferred tone): {voice_notes}

STRUCTURE — 5 short paragraphs, 8-15 words each opening, build longer
1. OPENING SCENE (1-2 short sentences). Drop the reader into the specific moment. No setup, no "Last week, I was thinking about..." — just into the room.
2. COMPLICATION. Something that didn't go as expected. A reaction you didn't anticipate. A constraint that wasn't supposed to bind.
3. THE PIVOT (1 sentence). The shift in your thinking. Often: "And then it hit me." (but don't say that.)
4. THE INSIGHT. The thing you now know. Phrased as something the reader can take, not as a declaration about you.
5. CLOSING. Either: a question that invites genuine response (not "What do you think?"), or a single sentence that doubles back to paragraph 1 differently.

FORMAT RULES
- 1500 characters max (LinkedIn hides past this in feed).
- Short paragraphs. White space matters in social feeds.
- One specific person mentioned by role (not name, unless you have permission).
- One specific number if relevant.
- NO bullet points. NO emojis (unless audience explicitly expects them).
- NO "I learned..." / "Here are 3 things..." openings.

BANNED PHRASES (these mark posts as low-effort)
"In today's fast-paced..."
"Game-changer"
"I'm humbled"
"Excited to share"
"Let's dive in"
"Here's the thing"
"Game on"
"Crushing it"

Now draft the post.

input_variables.name: insight
input_variables.example: Customer support reveals what your product actually is, not what you think it is

input_variables.name: moment
input_variables.example: Tuesday: support agent escalated a refund request that I realized signaled a real product gap we'd been ignoring

input_variables.name: audience
input_variables.example: B2B SaaS founders + early-stage product leads

input_variables.name: voice_notes
input_variables.example: Direct, slightly self-deprecating. Never use ‘grateful’ or ‘humbled’.

expected_output.sample: 5-paragraph LinkedIn post under 1500 chars with opening scene, complication, pivot, insight, and closing.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on narrative arc; resists banned phrases when listed.
Sometimes slips into ‘Here's what I learned’ structure; re-pin.
Tends toward overly polished tone; ask for ‘slightly unfinished feeling’.
Defaults to listicle; needs constant reminder.

variations.label: Carousel adaptation
variations.description: Adapt to 6-slide carousel.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace 5-paragraph structure with: ‘5-6 slides, each with a short title and 1-2 sentences. Slide 1 = opening scene; slide 6 = closing.’

variations.label: Story plus framework
variations.description: End by extracting a 3-point framework.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add after Insight paragraph: ‘then 3 bullets distilling the framework — short labels with a clause each.’

variations.label: Audience-specific reframe
variations.description: Same insight, multiple audiences.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also draft a 100-word version for {alt_audience} adjusting voice and stakes accordingly.’

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘open in the specific scene, not in a frame about the scene.’
Add: ‘the insight appears in paragraph 4 — earlier kills the arc.’
Add: ‘closing question must be specific enough that not everyone could answer it the same way.’
Add: ‘the protagonist of the story is NOT you. The insight emerges from the situation, not from your cleverness.’

faq.question: Should I include hashtags?
faq.answer: 1-3 specific ones max. 5+ generic hashtags (#leadership #growth) hurts more than helps. Leave room for the post to breathe.

faq.question: What about emojis?
faq.answer: Depends on your audience. Tech founders / consultants: avoid. Marketing / creators: sparingly. Match what already works for you, not what's trending.

faq.question: How is this different from blog writing?
faq.answer: LinkedIn rewards micro-arcs in 5 paragraphs. Blogs can be 5x longer. The opening matters more on LinkedIn — feed scrolling means you have 2 seconds.

meta_title: LinkedIn Post With Narrative Arc — Prompt
meta_description: 5-paragraph LinkedIn post anchored in a real moment: opening scene, complication, pivot, insight, closing. Banned-phrases list included.

title: One-Page Executive Summary
tldr: Compresses a long document (proposal, report, deck) into a one-page executive summary: situation, key findings, recommendation, and the 3 questions a CEO will ask.

use_cases.example: Compress vendor proposal to one page that surfaces tradeoffs, not just specs.
Q3 market analysis (50 pages) → 1-page exec summary with explicit recommendation.
Past month's product work compressed for board package.
M&A target analysis → 1 page focused on the few things that matter for the go/no-go.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the audience needs the full context (e.g., due diligence). Skip when the document is itself an executive summary — compressing twice loses meaning.
full_prompt:
You are compressing a long document into a one-page executive summary.

INPUT
- Full document (or detailed summary of it): {document}
- Audience and decision they're making: {audience_and_decision}

OUTPUT — ONE PAGE STRUCTURE

# {document_title} — Executive Summary

## Situation
3-5 sentences. What is this document about, why it exists, what decision it informs. Frame for the audience's actual concern.

## Key findings (3-5 bullets)
Each bullet is a complete sentence stating one finding with the evidence in parens. NOT topic headers.
Example: "Customer churn dropped 23% post-feature-X, but only for accounts with >5 seats — suggesting feature is enterprise-relevant only."

## Recommendation
One paragraph with the recommendation stated upfront, then the reasoning. If alternatives were considered, mention them. If the recommendation is conditional, name the condition.

## What we don't know
2-3 things that remain uncertain. This signals intellectual honesty AND surfaces what the decision depends on.

## Questions the CEO will ask
3 questions you predict they'll ask, with one-line answers. Pick questions you can answer; if they'd ask one you can't, note it explicitly.

## Reading guide
A one-line "read pages X-Y for the full reasoning on the key finding."

RULES
- ONE PAGE. About 500 words. Cut anything that doesn't drive toward the decision.
- No background information unless it directly affects the recommendation.
- Numbers > adjectives. "23%" not "significant".
- Don't restate obvious facts the audience already knows.
- Hedge LESS, not more, than the underlying document — the audience needs a position to react to.

DOCUMENT
{document}

AUDIENCE & DECISION
{audience_and_decision}

Now write the summary.

input_variables.name: document_title
input_variables.description: Title of the doc being summarized
input_variables.example: Q3 Market Analysis: Mid-Market Vector DB Opportunity

input_variables.name: document
input_variables.example: [paste the full report]

input_variables.name: audience_and_decision
input_variables.description: Who's reading and what they're deciding
input_variables.example: CEO + board. Decision: greenlight $4M investment in mid-market sales team.

expected_output.sample: Six headed sections totaling ~500 words: situation, findings, recommendation, unknowns, predicted questions, reading guide.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at compression and at predicting CEO questions.
Very good; occasionally bloats recommendation paragraph.
Strong on findings; recommendation can be wishy-washy — re-pin ‘commit to one position’.
Tends to restate the whole document; needs aggressive cap on word count.

variations.label: Two-version
variations.description: Generate both a 1-page and a 1-paragraph TL;DR.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also write a 50-word TL;DR for use in email subject preview / Slack DM.’

variations.label: Decision matrix-led
variations.description: Open with a 4-cell decision matrix.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace Situation section with: ‘a 2x2 matrix of options with 1-line characterization each, then the recommendation references the matrix.’

variations.label: Risk-focused
variations.description: Emphasize risks over opportunities.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add new section before Recommendation: ‘TOP RISKS: 3 risks with severity and mitigation each.’

failure_modes.fix: Hard cap: ‘exactly 500 words ± 10%; trim until you fit.’

failure_modes.symptom: Findings are topic headers, not findings.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘each finding is a complete sentence stating an insight, not a label.’
Add: ‘state the recommendation in one declarative sentence at the start of the paragraph. Then defend.’

failure_modes.symptom: ‘What we don't know’ is missing or generic.
failure_modes.fix: Force: ‘at least 2 specific unknowns; vague ones (‘we need more data’) don't count.’

faq.question: Why include ‘what we don't know’?
faq.answer: Two reasons: signals epistemic honesty (executives trust it more), and surfaces what additional work would close the decision gap.

faq.question: How do I handle multiple recommendations?
faq.answer: Pick one primary recommendation. Mention 1-2 alternates briefly in the recommendation paragraph; don't equally weight.

faq.question: What if the document is itself a summary?
faq.answer: Don't summarize a summary. Either present the original summary as-is, or expand a section and summarize that.

meta_title: One-Page Executive Summary — Prompt
meta_description: Compress a long document to one page: situation, findings, recommendation, unknowns, predicted CEO questions, reading guide. ~500 words.

title: Rewrite Same Content For Different Audience
tldr: Re-versions the same content for a different audience — adjusts vocabulary, examples, stakes, length — while preserving the core argument. Useful for cross-functional comms.

use_cases.scenario: Engineering RFC → product update for execs
use_cases.example: Same proposal, different framing: technical RFC becomes 2-paragraph email for the leadership team.
Internal release notes → customer-friendly email.

use_cases.scenario: Research finding → ELI5 for company-wide all-hands
use_cases.example: ML research result → accessible 5-minute talk.
Same numbers, different stakes and tone for the team.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the original was already audience-targeted (you'll dilute it). Skip when the audiences need different INFORMATION, not different FRAMING — that's editing, not re-versioning.
full_prompt:
You are rewriting the same content for a different audience. The argument stays the same; the framing changes.

INPUT
- Original content: {original}
- Original audience: {original_audience}
- New audience: {new_audience}
- New audience's core concern: {their_concern}
- New stake (what they care about): {stake}

YOUR PROCESS

## Step 1: Extract the core argument
In one sentence, what is the original ARGUING? (Not just ‘about’ — what's the position?)

## Step 2: Re-frame
- Vocabulary: replace jargon with terms the new audience uses. If your new audience uses different jargon, use theirs.
- Examples: swap or remove examples that won't land. Add examples relevant to {new_audience}.
- Stakes: lead with what the new audience cares about ({stake}), not what the original led with.
- Length: usually shorter for executives, longer for newcomers. Default: 40-60% of original.

## Step 3: Write the new version
Headline + content. Use the new audience's expected format (email if they're execs, doc if engineers, slide bullets if it's for a meeting).

## Step 4: What's lost
2-3 bullets: things the original conveyed that the new version can't. Useful to either accept the loss or to attach as appendix.

CRITICAL CONSTRAINTS
- Preserve the core argument exactly. If the original says X, the new version says X (in different words).
- Don't invent new claims the original didn't make.
- Don't soften the argument unless the new audience is genuinely sensitive — most audiences want directness.
- If the original is wrong about something, flag it; don't silently fix it.

ORIGINAL CONTENT
{original}

Now do Steps 1-4.

input_variables.name: original
input_variables.example: [paste the original]

input_variables.name: original_audience
input_variables.example: Senior engineers on the platform team

input_variables.name: new_audience
input_variables.description: Who the new version is for
input_variables.example: CTO + 2-3 engineering directors

input_variables.name: their_concern
input_variables.example: Cost, scaling risk, team-impact

input_variables.name: stake
input_variables.description: What makes this matter to them
input_variables.example: $X spend over 12 months, plus delivery risk on Q3 roadmap

expected_output.sample: Steps 1-4: core argument in one sentence, re-framing notes, the rewritten content, and a ‘what's lost’ bullets list.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at preserving the argument while changing register.
Very good; can over-soften for non-technical audiences — re-pin directness.
Handles vocabulary swap well; weaker on adjusting stakes.
Tends to rephrase rather than re-frame; needs explicit step 1.

variations.label: Multi-audience same call
variations.description: Generate 3 versions for 3 audiences.
variations.prompt_snippet: Accept list of audiences; produce one re-version each plus comparison table.

variations.label: Slide-format output
variations.description: Output as slide bullets.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace prose output with: ‘5-7 slides, each with title + 2-4 bullets.’

variations.label: Tone audit before rewrite
variations.description: Diagnose tone of original first.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Step 0: ‘Diagnose original tone: register, certainty, formality. Then in Step 2 explicitly note what changes.’

failure_modes.symptom: New version has a different argument.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘same argument, different framing’ and add ‘before writing, paste the one-sentence argument; verify the new version says the same thing.’

failure_modes.symptom: Over-softened (executive version doesn't take a position).
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘the new audience wants the call; soften the JUSTIFICATION wording, not the recommendation itself.’
Add: ‘use the NEW audience's natural terms even if they're imprecise from the original's view; that's the point.’
Add: ‘each loss must cite a specific phrase or example from the original.’

faq.question: When should I lengthen vs shorten?
faq.answer: Lengthen for less-familiar audiences (more context). Shorten for senior audiences (less context, more conclusion). Default to shortening; over-explanation is the more common error.

faq.question: How do I avoid the new version sounding patronizing?
faq.answer: Don't define terms the new audience knows. Don't over-justify. Don't say ‘in simple terms’ — just BE simple.

faq.question: What if the original is technically wrong?
faq.answer: Flag it in Step 1 (‘core argument is X, but I notice claim Y is unsupported in the original’). Don't silently fix — that's a separate decision.

meta_title: Rewrite Same Content For Different Audience — Prompt
meta_description: Re-version content for a new audience: same argument, different vocabulary/examples/stakes. Plus a ‘what's lost’ list to capture compression tradeoffs.

title: Blog Post from Outline (voice-matched + sourced)
tldr: Expand a bullet-point outline into a 800-1500 word blog post matching a sample-voice. Includes specific examples, real-world numbers, and 'so what' takeaways per section.
full_prompt:
You write blog posts that don't read like AI wrote them. Voice-match a reference sample. Don't pad. Every section earns its place.

INPUTS:
- outline: bullet-point outline (sections + key points)
- target_length: word count target
- voice_sample: 1-2 paragraphs from the same author for voice anchor
- audience: who reads this
- avoid_phrases: list of phrases the author never uses

STRUCTURE:
1. Hook (50-100 words): specific opening — a number, a moment, a contrarian claim. NOT 'In today's fast-paced world...'.
2. Body sections (per outline bullet): each ends with a 1-sentence 'so what' that ties to the audience's reality.
3. Examples (every claim): pull from the outline; if outline has no examples, generate concrete ones (named companies, real numbers, specific dates).
4. Closer (75-150 words): one thought worth taking away, not a recap.

VOICE-MATCH RULES:
- Sentence-length variance: match the sample's mix of short + long sentences.
- Vocabulary: contractions or not? Technical terms or plain? Match.
- Authority markers (e.g., 'in my experience', 'we shipped X') — preserve or omit based on sample.

FORBIDDEN (always, even if not in avoid_phrases):
- 'leverage', 'unlock', 'unleash', 'game-changing', 'next-level', 'in today's fast-paced world'
- 'Let me show you...', 'Here's the thing:', 'But here's the kicker:'
- Hashtag-style intro questions ('Tired of X?')

Begin.

input_variables.name: outline
input_variables.example:
- Why we moved off seat-based pricing
- The 3 surprises
- What we'd do differently
- Concrete metrics 6mo in

input_variables.name: voice_sample
input_variables.example: We don't use the word 'leverage' here. We say 'use'. ...

input_variables.name: audience
input_variables.example: SaaS founders at $1-10M ARR

input_variables.name: avoid_phrases
input_variables.example: ['game-changer', 'tech stack']

expected_output.sample:
# Why we killed seat-based pricing (6 months in)

In April we removed per-seat pricing for accounts above $50k ARR. By October, ARR was up 23% and churn was flat. Here's what surprised us — and what we'd do differently...

## The 3 surprises
### Surprise 1: Most teams didn't add seats they were 'saving'
[concrete example with numbers]

So what: if you're nervous that usage-based will cap revenue, the cap was probably imaginary.
...

use_cases.example: Outline of 4 bullets → 1200-word post in author's voice.
Lead engineer's outline → polished draft for review.
Ghostwriter feeds outline + 3 prior posts as voice samples; generates v1 the named author edits.
Sales-engineering team has bullet-form case study; this prompt produces the marketing-ready version.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for technical reference docs — different prompt territory. Also skip for highly personal essays where the author's specific phrasing is the point.

few_shot_examples.input: outline: '- Moved off K8s after 2 years - Why - What we use now - Lessons'. voice_sample: 'We picked K8s in 2022 because everyone said to. Two years later we ripped it out. Here's what happened.' length: 800. audience: 'eng leaders'
few_shot_examples.output: [Direct opener with specific date + claim, body sections each ending with operational lesson, closer that's a single observation worth taking away — not a recap. 820 words, ~12 short and 8 medium sentences matching sample rhythm.]

model_compatibility.notes: Best at voice-match and avoiding AI clichés.
Reliable default for most posts.
Reaches for 'in today's fast-paced world' if not blocked; reinforce forbidden list.
Workable; expect to manually punch up 2-3 sections.

variations.label: Twitter-thread companion
variations.description: Generate a 10-tweet thread summarizing.
variations.prompt_snippet: After the post, generate a 10-tweet thread (240 chars each) that pulls the most quotable lines. Tweet 1 hooks, tweet 10 links to the post.

variations.label: Newsletter format
variations.description: Shorter, more direct.
variations.prompt_snippet: Target_length override: 500-700 words. Add a 'TL;DR' at top (2 sentences). Newsletter readers skim.

variations.label: SEO-aware
variations.description: Hit a target keyword naturally.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: target_keyword. Use the keyword in title, H2, and 3-5 places in body. Never stuff.

failure_modes.fix: Every section needs a 'so what' — if you can't write one, cut the section

failure_modes.symptom: AI clichés slip through ('in this article we'll explore')
failure_modes.fix: Forbidden phrases list enforced; reject and rewrite

failure_modes.symptom: Voice drifts to corporate-speak in body
failure_modes.fix: Re-check voice_sample mid-draft; rewrite any paragraph that doesn't match rhythm

failure_modes.symptom: Generic examples ('many companies have found that...')
failure_modes.fix: Examples must be specific — named, dated, numbered. If outline lacks specifics, generate plausible ones explicitly marked '[example]'

faq.question: How much voice-match can I expect?
faq.answer: ~70-85% match on rhythm and vocab choices. Won't catch the author's specific obsessions or running jokes — humans still edit.

faq.question: What length is the sweet spot?
faq.answer: 800-1500 words for blog. <500 is newsletter. >2000 starts losing reader attention without strong narrative.

faq.question: Can it write multiple posts in a series?
faq.answer: Yes — pass previous_posts as context to maintain voice continuity. Cap at 5 priors (more = context bloat).

meta_title: Blog Post from Outline — Voice-Matched, No AI Clichés
meta_description: Expand a bullet outline to 800-1500 word blog post matching a sample voice. Specific examples, 'so what' per section, forbidden-phrase list.

title: Email Reply Drafter (tone-matched)
tldr: Draft an email reply matching the original sender's tone (formal/casual/terse), addressing every question raised, with clear next steps and no fluff openings.
full_prompt:
You draft email replies that read like the user wrote them. Match the sender's tone — formal email gets a formal reply, terse Slack-style gets terse. Address every question they asked. No openings like 'Thanks for reaching out!' unless the sample shows that.

INPUTS:
- incoming_email: the email being replied to
- user_voice_sample (optional): 1-2 prior emails the user wrote, to match their voice
- desired_outcome: what the user wants this reply to achieve
- constraints (optional): things to mention or avoid (e.g., 'don't commit to a date')

PROCEDURE:
1. Identify every question or ask in the incoming email.
2. Match tone of incoming email:
   - Formal (Mr/Ms, complete sentences): reply formal
   - Casual (Hi <first name>, contractions): reply casual
   - Terse (no greeting, one line): reply terse
3. Address each question/ask with a direct sentence. No padding.
4. End with clear next step OR 'no action needed'. Don't end with 'Let me know if you have questions!' unless sample shows that pattern.
5. Sign-off matches the sample's style.

RULES:
- Never open with 'Hope this email finds you well' or 'Thanks for your email'.
- If user voice unknown, default to: short greeting, direct answers, brief sign-off.
- 50-200 words for most replies. 200-400 for complex multi-question emails. Never over 400.
- Don't apologize for delays unless sample shows that pattern.

Begin.

input_variables.name: incoming_email
input_variables.example: Hi Jordan, two questions: (1) Can we move our weekly to Tuesdays at 3pm? (2) Did you see my note about the Q3 budget? Thanks, Sam

input_variables.name: user_voice_sample
input_variables.example: Hey Sarah — yes that works. I'll send the draft Friday. — Jordan

input_variables.name: desired_outcome
input_variables.example: Accept the Tuesday slot, ask for more time on the budget

input_variables.name: constraints
input_variables.example: ['Don't commit to a date for the budget review yet']

expected_output.sample: Hey Sam — yes Tuesdays at 3pm works. On the Q3 budget: I saw the note but haven't been able to dig in yet. Let me get back to you by end of next week. — Jordan

use_cases.example: User flags emails for AI draft; this prompt produces drafts in user's voice for one-click send.
EA pastes incoming + brief on what to say; gets first draft in exec's voice.
Follow-up after first cold email — match the original's tone.
Manager replying to escalated thread — sound human, not corporate.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for sensitive HR / legal emails — these need human review for liability. Also skip for emails where the user disagrees with the desired_outcome and is venting (the tone won't be right).

few_shot_examples.input: incoming: 'Where's the data?' (terse). desired: send the link. voice_sample: 'Here. — J'
few_shot_examples.output: Here: drive.example.com/q3-data. — J

model_compatibility.notes: Best at tone-match without over-formalizing.
Recommended for production — fast, cheap, voice-matches well with sample.
Slightly more formal default; reinforce voice rules.
Workable for casual replies.

variations.label: No-commit mode
variations.description: Acknowledge without committing to anything.
variations.prompt_snippet: Constraint added: 'Don't commit to dates, numbers, or decisions. Reply with acknowledgment + offer to follow up.'

variations.label: Multi-language
variations.description: Reply in same language as incoming.
variations.prompt_snippet: Detect incoming_email language. Reply in same language. Voice rules apply within that language.

variations.label: Decline-politely mode
variations.description: Say no without being a pushover or apologetic.
variations.prompt_snippet: Decline the request directly + offer one alternative if one exists. Don't apologize 3 times. Don't pretend it's a scheduling issue if the answer is just no.

failure_modes.symptom: Opens with 'Thanks for reaching out!' when sender didn't initiate
failure_modes.fix: Drop generic openings; match sample style

failure_modes.symptom: Adds 'Let me know if you have any questions!' to terse replies
failure_modes.fix: Match sender's sign-off pattern; terse → terse

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't answer one of the questions
failure_modes.fix: Procedure step 1 — list every question; verify each is addressed

failure_modes.symptom: Padded reply (200 words when 50 would do)
failure_modes.fix: Length-cap by complexity of incoming email; default to 'shortest that's complete'

faq.question: How many voice samples do I need?
faq.answer: 1-3 is plenty. More starts diluting — your voice across many contexts varies.

faq.question: Can it handle multi-thread context?
faq.answer: Pass the thread as incoming_email; it'll address the latest message but use thread for context.

faq.question: What about emojis?
faq.answer: Match the sender. If they use 👋 you can use 👋. If they don't, don't add.

meta_title: Email Reply Drafter — Tone-Matched, Direct, No Fluff
meta_description: Draft email replies matching sender tone (formal/casual/terse). Address every question. No 'Thanks for reaching out!' openers.

title: Headline Rewriter (5x Stronger)
tldr: Rewrite a weak headline into 5 stronger options across distinct angles (specificity, contrarian, benefit, curiosity, urgency). Surface which one fits which context.
full_prompt:
You rewrite weak headlines into 5 distinct strong versions. Different angles let the user pick what fits the context.

INPUTS:
- weak_headline: the original
- about: 1-sentence description of what the post/page is
- audience: who reads this
- character_limit: max chars (default 70)
- forbidden_words (optional)

GENERATE 5 versions, one per angle:
1. SPECIFIC: replace vague words with concrete numbers/names
2. CONTRARIAN: take the opposite stance of the obvious framing
3. BENEFIT-LED: lead with outcome for the reader, not the topic
4. CURIOSITY: tease without spoiling
5. URGENT: time/scarcity (only if real)

FOR EACH: 1-line label + the headline + 1-line 'when this fits' note.

RULES:
- Under character_limit each.
- No 'How to...', 'X tips for...', 'The ultimate guide to...' — these read as listicle clickbait.
- Don't make every version about you (avoid 'we', 'our' unless natural).
- Real urgency only — don't invent scarcity.

Begin.

input_variables.name: weak_headline
input_variables.example: Why You Should Use Vector Databases for AI Apps

input_variables.name: about
input_variables.example: Our 6-month experience migrating from Postgres to Qdrant for RAG at 10M vectors

input_variables.name: audience
input_variables.example: Engineering leads building RAG

input_variables.name: forbidden_words
input_variables.example: ['unleash','revolutionize']

expected_output.sample:
1. **Specific:** "Why we moved 10M vectors off Postgres after 6 months"
   _Best when the audience needs proof you've actually done it._

2. **Contrarian:** "Postgres + pgvector is fine. Until it isn't."
   _Use when readers are pgvector-loyal and need to be unsettled._

3. **Benefit-led:** "Cut RAG p99 latency from 850ms to 80ms"
   _For latency-sensitive readers; leads with the metric._

4. **Curiosity:** "The vector-DB choice we wish we'd made 6 months earlier"
   _For browse-mode readers; works in newsletters._

5. **Urgent:** "Five vector DBs to evaluate before your next sprint"
   _Only if there's a real timeline pressure for the audience._

use_cases.example: Run on every draft; pick the version that fits the channel (Twitter vs newsletter vs blog).
5 versions become 5 subject-line cohorts.
Quarterly hero re-test; rotate one of these in.
Same episode, 5 angle options; pick what fits show's voice.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for regulated industries where every headline needs legal review. Also skip for evergreen academic content where standard descriptive titles are expected.

few_shot_examples.input: weak: 'How to Make a Better Resume'. about: 'data on resume formats that get interviews in tech'. audience: 'job-seekers in tech'
few_shot_examples.output:
1. Specific: "7 resume formats we tested across 1,200 tech apps — only 2 worked"
2. Contrarian: "Your resume is fine. Recruiters skip you for a different reason."
3. Benefit: "Cut your time-to-first-interview in half"
4. Curiosity: "The one resume change that doubled callback rates"
5. Urgent: "Resume changes that matter before May 1 hiring freeze"

model_compatibility.notes: Best at the 5-angle variety without repetition.
Strong at specificity + contrarian.
Cheap for bulk; sometimes mild.
Workable; expect to manually punch 1-2.

variations.label: 10 variations
variations.description: Double the output across 10 angles.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add 5 more angles: data-led, controversial, personal-anecdote, list-style, question-form. 10 total.

variations.label: SEO-targeted
variations.description: Include target keyword.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT target_keyword. Every variation must include the keyword naturally (not stuffed).

variations.label: Subject-line mode
variations.description: Email-specific.
variations.prompt_snippet: Character limit 40. Optimize for inbox preview. Lowercase preferred.

failure_modes.fix: 5 distinct angles; reject if two are too close

failure_modes.symptom: Defaults to clickbait formulas ('You Won't Believe...')
failure_modes.fix: Forbidden patterns list; reject clickbait shapes
Specificity needs a concrete number/name from the about input
Strict count; regenerate the offending one

faq.question: Which angle usually wins?
faq.answer: Specificity for blog/SEO. Curiosity for newsletter. Benefit for landing pages. Contrarian for social. Urgent if there's a real deadline.

faq.question: Should I A/B test all 5?
faq.answer: No — pick 2-3 you'd actually use, test those. Spreading too thin produces noise.

faq.question: What if none of the 5 feel right?
faq.answer: Re-run with a sharper 'about' input. Vague inputs produce mediocre headlines.

meta_title: Headline Rewriter — 5 Stronger Versions, 5 Angles
meta_description: Turn a weak headline into 5 distinct strong versions across specific/contrarian/benefit/curiosity/urgency. With context fit notes.

title: Tighten Prose by 30% (preserve meaning)
tldr: Cut a draft by ~30% without losing any claim or example. Returns the tightened version + a list of every cut and the reason.
full_prompt:
You tighten prose. Remove redundancy, hedging, and padding. Preserve every claim and example.

INPUTS:
- draft: the original text
- target_reduction_pct: how much to cut (default 30%)
- preserve_voice (bool): if true, match the author's rhythm; if false, aim for maximum density

PROCEDURE:
1. Read the draft. Count words.
2. Cut targets:
   - Hedging: 'I think', 'it seems', 'arguably', 'perhaps' (remove or commit)
   - Throat-clearing: 'It's important to note that', 'As we'll see', 'In this section'
   - Redundant adjectives: 'completely free', 'absolutely critical', 'each and every'
   - Filler clauses: 'in order to' → 'to'; 'due to the fact that' → 'because'
   - Repeated points (consolidate into one)
   - 'There is/are' constructions where direct verb works
3. Preserve all: claims, examples, numbers, names, conclusions.
4. Output tightened version + diff showing cuts with 1-line reason per cut.

RULES:
- Don't reorganize structure — same paragraphs, same order.
- Don't change voice (if preserve_voice true).
- If you can't cut to target without losing signal, output current best + explanation.

Begin.

input_variables.name: draft
input_variables.example: It's important to note that, in our experience, the use of cache has, in many cases, completely eliminated the previously observed latency issues that were affecting our users...

input_variables.name: preserve_voice
input_variables.example: true

expected_output.sample:
## Tightened (32% shorter — 410 → 280 words)

Caching eliminated the latency issues our users hit...

## Cuts
- 'It's important to note that' → removed (throat-clearing)
- 'in our experience' → kept once at the top, removed 3 other instances (repeated hedge)
- 'in many cases' → removed (hedge)
- 'completely eliminated' → 'eliminated' (redundant intensifier)
- 'the previously observed latency issues that were affecting' → 'the latency issues' (filler clause)

use_cases.example: Run on every draft before publishing; typical 25-35% reduction with no signal loss.
Long reply → tight reply. Cuts hedging that makes emails feel uncertain.
Long async update → punchy version coworkers will actually read.
Speaker notes too long for screen; cut to deliverable in 30 seconds.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for legal/regulatory text where hedging is intentional. Also skip for fiction where rhythm and pacing rely on 'redundancy'.

few_shot_examples.input: draft: 'It is generally believed by many experts that there is a possibility that the use of caching mechanisms could potentially help reduce the latency.' target: 30%
few_shot_examples.output:
Tightened: 'Caching reduces latency.'
Cuts: 'It is generally believed by many experts that' (throat-clearing), 'there is a possibility' (hedge), 'could potentially' (double hedge), 'the use of...mechanisms' (filler).

model_compatibility.notes: Best at preserving meaning during aggressive cuts.
Cheap + reliable for production editing pipelines.
Strong; sometimes too aggressive.
Workable for English; weaker on subtle hedging cuts.

variations.label: Aggressive 50%
variations.description: When you really need it shorter.
variations.prompt_snippet: Target_reduction_pct: 50. Acknowledge some signal loss may occur; flag any cuts that risk meaning.

variations.label: Hedge-only
variations.description: Only remove hedges, nothing else.
variations.prompt_snippet: Only touch hedges and qualifiers. Leave structure and examples untouched.

variations.label: Translation-prep
variations.description: Pre-tighten before machine translation.
variations.prompt_snippet: Optimize for translation: short sentences, no idioms, no 'whose'/'which' clauses. Cuts often >40%.

failure_modes.fix: Preservation rule mandatory; if asked to cut and only claims remain, return 'cannot cut further without signal loss'
Same paragraph order; same paragraph count
Preserve_voice flag enforces match

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't show cuts (just returns shortened version)
failure_modes.fix: Always output cut diff for transparency

faq.question: Is 30% realistic?
faq.answer: Yes for first drafts. Edited drafts may only have 10-15% to cut. If a draft has 50% to cut, the draft is in trouble structurally.

faq.question: Will it strip my personality?
faq.answer: With preserve_voice=true, no. It cuts padding, not personality. Personality lives in word choice and rhythm, not in 'in our experience'.

faq.question: Can it tighten code comments?
faq.answer: Yes — pass the comment block as draft. Especially good at cutting 'TODO: figure out a better approach maybe'.

meta_title: Tighten Prose by 30% — Preserve Every Claim
meta_description: Cut drafts by ~30% without losing claims or examples. Targets hedging, throat-clearing, filler clauses. Diff per cut.
